title-group.article-title: Policy solutions to ameliorate the negative impact of aging on economic productivity: a systematic review

contrib-group.aff: aInstitute for Health & Aging, Department of Social and Behavioral Sciences, University of California, San Francisco, San Francisco, CA, USA
bInstitute for Global Health Sciences, University of California, San Francisco, San Francisco, CA, USA
cHealth, Nutrition and Population Global Practice, The World Bank, Washington, D.C., USA

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Population aging creates fiscal pressures on public systems for health care, pensions, and social protection. Identifying policies to mitigate the negative impact of an aging population on economic growth and productivity is crucial.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: Adhering to PRISMA standards, we systematically searched four databases based on concepts of (1) population aging, (2) economic growth, (3) economic productivity, (4) policy, (5) intervention, and (6) amelioration or improvement. Included studies were in English and assessed how policy interventions affect economic growth or productivity.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Six studies were identified, modeling policy interventions aimed at increasing labor force participation to reduce the impact of aging on economic productivity. Policies focused on extrinsic motivation, such as mandating workers to remain in the labor force, might boost participation, but may reduce productivity. In contrast, policies enhancing intrinsic motivation through improvements in health, education, and skill development positively influenced labor force retention by increasing human capital.

sec.title: Discussion and conclusion
sec.p: Evidence from a small number of modeling studies suggests that policies that foster intrinsic motivation should be prioritized, as these approaches promote a more sustainable workforce while avoiding the potentially negative consequences of extrinsic motivation policies like mandating labor force participation by older adult workers.

list-item.p: First systematic review to assess policy effects on GDP amid global population aging.
Policies classified by intrinsic vs. extrinsic motivation frameworks.
Extrinsic motivation may raise participation at the cost of decreased productivity.
Intrinsic motivation-based policies may offer more sustainable solutions.
Human capital investment is linked to long-term gains in economic productivity.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: With advances in healthcare and living standards resulting in longer life expectancies, population aging is becoming increasingly prevalent worldwide: the population 65 and over is now the fastest-growing age group in the world (United Nations, 2019). With this demographic shift, global life expectancy is projected to reach 77 years by 2050 (United Nations, 2019). For this reason, many countries are expecting the potential support ratio, which compares the number of working-age persons to those over 65, to drop below 2 (United Nations, 2019).

Population age structure has implications for economic productivity and development (Bloom et al., 2010, 2015; Hall & Jones, 1999) and impacts both labor market supply (e.g., the number of workers available in the workforce) and demand (e.g., the number of workers needed to provide long-term care, elder care, or child care) (Huang et al., 2019). Population aging also impacts economic performance in terms of the capacity of the workforce to complete various tasks (Maestas et al., 2023; Skirbekk, 2008). Critically, population aging contributes to fiscal pressures associated with the simultaneous shrinking of the workforce, reduction in the support ratio, and increasing need to build and reinforce public systems for health care, pensions, and social protection for older persons (United Nations, 2019).

Anecdotal evidence—such as the retirement age–related protests in France in 2023— indicates that a brute force approach to ensure labor market supply by simply raising the retirement age may not always be politically viable (Breeden, 2023). When such a policy is legalized, ratified, and implemented, evidence suggests that it may mitigate the negative impact of an aging population on economic growth (Lai & Yip, 2022). However, even when such policies are politically viable, the benefits gained may come at the expense of overall labor productivity and well-being (Gawthorpe, 2022). Collectively, the evidence raises questions about the sustainability of this approach in reversing current support ratio trend.

Drawing from the self-determination theory (SDT) framework, we posit that sustainable and successful policies will be those that focus not only on extrinsic motivation but also on intrinsic motivation (Deci, 1971; Dysvik & Kuvaas, 2010; Miao et al., 2020). Intrinsic motivation refers to reasons that individuals initiate an activity because it is interesting and satisfying in itself—including autonomy, mastery, and purpose (Deci, 1971). Intrinsic motivation is recognized to drive productivity, innovation, and labor supply decisions. Extrinsic motivation refers to reasons that individuals engage in an activity to achieve an external goal (e.g., a larger pension, monetary rewards); such as wages or taxes (Deci, 1971). Extrinsic motivation influences participation and effort. Integrating motivational theory into macroeconomic analysis provides a framework for understanding how preference incentives shape behavioral responses. SDT postulates that humans are prone to personal growth and learning, but that these tendencies require conditions that support autonomy, competence, and relatedness (Ryan and Deci, 2000, 2020).

Policies operating through intrinsic and extrinsic motivation influence macroeconomic outcomes via sequential labor-market mechanisms. Motivation shapes individual behaviors, which subsequently impact broader macroeconomic outcomes. At the individual level, policies that enhance autonomy, competence, and security (intrinsic) or alter financial incentives (extrinsic) shape labor supply decisions, including retirement timing, hours worked, and participation in later life. Sustained participation and engagement may, in turn, affect skill retention and worker effort, influencing productivity. These behavioral responses aggregate across the workforce to shape total labor input and output, ultimately affecting economy-wide indicators such as GDP and GDP per capita.

Challenges to identifying and implementing effective policies suggest that high-yield policies may require consideration of individual psychology and psychosocial well-being as well as the incorporation of individual preferences and needs. Requiring individuals to prolong their working life will probably necessitate more than monetary and extrinsic rewards; instead, cultivating an intrinsically grounded preference to remain in the workforce may be essential. Studies have demonstrated the powerful impact of intrinsic motivation on worker behaviors in various settings including healthcare quality(Lagarde et al., 2019), innovative work behaviors for knowledge workers (Venketsamy & Lew, 2022), and retention of in-service teachers (Alexander et al., 2020).

With the foundational understanding derived from the SDT framework, we conducted a systematic literature review to identify evidence of policy interventions intended to ameliorate the effects of aging populations on economic productivity. Our primary aim was to summarize and categorize the types of policy levers that have been attempted in various countries and to review the effect of these policies on mitigating the economic impact of aging. Given the uncertainty regarding acceptable, feasible, and viable policies for addressing the negative impact of population aging on economic growth and productivity, identifying and evaluating policy solutions that may mitigate the negative impact of aging on the economy while circumventing potential policy-related externalities is critical. The information generated through this review may inform future evidence-based policy making in diverse settings.

sec.title: Study design
sec.p:
This systematic literature review was conducted following Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) reporting standards (Liberati et al., 2009). We systematically searched four databases (EconLit, PAIS Index, ProQuest, and Web of Science) and identified published evidence on policies impacting economic productivity amid an aging population. The database selection reflects our focus on macroeconomic and modeling studies, which are primarily published in economics and policy journals. The following concepts were used in the development of a search string: (1) population aging, (2) economic growth, (3) economic productivity, (4) policy, (5) intervention, and (6) amelioration or improvement. Search terms were entered in English. No language restrictions were applied at the search stage; however, only studies published in English were included. We provided an example of terms utilized in our search in Table 1. See Supplementary 1 for the full search strategy. No ethical approval was required for this desk-based study.Table 1
Sample search strings.Table 1
ProQuest Search Strings
1(population ag?ing OR ag?ing labo?r force OR ag?ing)
2(economic growth OR productivity OR production OR growth rate per capita OR total factor
3productivity OR gross domestic product per capita OR GDP OR growth rate per capita or growth rate)
4(ameliorate OR reduce OR improve OR contain OR minimi?e OR limit OR restrict)
51 AND 2 AND 3 AND 4

sec.title: Eligibility criteria
sec.p:
A study was eligible for inclusion in this review if it was published in English and evaluated the impact of a policy, as an intervention or an independent variable, on economic growth or economic productivity as an outcome variable. We defined economic growth and productivity as gross domestic product (GDP) or GDP per capita, or GDP per worker, but did not exclude studies that reported other quantitative macro outcomes such as employment rates of older workers, labor-productivity indices, or output per hour worked. We focused on quantitative analyses, examining policies that may mitigate declines in economic growth and productivity in the context of an aging population post–Second World War (after 1945) in any country worldwide. Because we aimed to extract quantitative outcomes, we excluded qualitative studies as well as studies with only a theoretical model and without empirical evidence. Similarly, gray literature, conference presentations, abstracts, protocols, commentaries, and editorials were excluded from our review. Literature reviews and meta-analyses were not included in our final review, but their references were searched for potentially appropriate studies. An overview of our inclusion and exclusion criteria is presented in Table 2.Table 2
PICOs inclusion and exclusion.Table 2
InclusionExclusion
Population•
Countries experiencing post- Second World War (after 1945) demographic aging•
Studies unrelated to aging population dynamics
Intervention•
Policies with impact on economic growth or economic productivity•
Studies not examining a policy intervention
Comparator•
Pre-policy periods, alternative polities or no policy/exposure conditions•
None
Outcomes•
Quantitative economic outcomes, specifically:-
Gross domestic product (GDP)-
GDP per capita-
GDP per worker•
Any measure capturing economic growth or economic productivity•
Studies without quantitative economic outcomes•
Studies measuring unrelated outcomes (e.g., health outcomes only, demographic trends without economic metrics)
Study Design•
Quantitative empirical studies•
Studies evaluating the impact of a policy (as intervention or independent variable) on economic outcomes•
Qualitative studies•
Theoretical models without empirical evidence•
Conference abstracts, presentations, commentaries, editorials, protocols•
Gray literature•
Literature reviews or meta-analyses (though references were screened)•
Non-peer reveiewed material
Other•
Published in English•
Non English studies

sec.title: Risk of bias assessment
sec.p: The Mixed Methods Appraisal Tool (MMAT) (Hong et al., 2018) was applied to assess the methodological quality of included studies. The MMAT includes seven criteria, based on study design, to inform how well an empirical article has addressed elements required for minimum quality. However, several MMAT domains are designed primarily for empirical primary-data studies and may not fully capture strengths and limitations specific to macro-simulation models. In particular, criteria related to sampling representativeness and nonresponse bias are only partially applicable to modeling studies that rely on secondary macroeconomic datasets and calibrated structural parameters. Therefore, the findings do not necessarily indicate weak internal validity but instead reflect partial misalignment between the appraisal tool and the modeling paradigm. Scores from this assessment can be found in Online Appendix Table 1.

sec.title: Data extraction and analysis
sec.p: Studies were first examined by title and abstract. Studies that did not meet the inclusion criteria in the title and abstract stage were removed, and the full-text of the remaining articles was screened again for eligibility. All references within the included studies were checked to identify additional relevant articles. We used the Covidence review software (Veritas Health Innovation, Melbourne, Australia, available at www.covidence.org) to manage our review, specifically for title and abstract screening, as well as for full-text screening. The software automatically removed duplicate studies, and the study team then manually checked again for duplicates. For each of the studies, two of the three reviewers independently screened studies for eligibility. We then extracted key details from studies using a predetermined 16-item matrix. We used Microsoft Excel (Microsoft Corporation, Redmond, WA, USA)(Microsoft Corp, 2019) for the assessment of risk of bias and data extraction. Conflicts were resolved through discussion between the reviewers and resolved by a third reviewer when necessary.

Study outcomes were first summarized in a narrative synthesis. Results were then categorized by type of policy using a self-determination theory framework to classify the individual behavior motivation mechanism related to the policies identified in our review (Miao et al., 2020; Sansone & Harackiewicz, 2012; Welters et al., 2014).

sec.title: Limitations
sec.p: As in many fields, published studies have a tendency to report positive results (instead of null results), which may lead to publication bias in systematic reviews. The database used in our review reflects our focus on macroeconomic and CGE modeling studies, however this does leave a possibility that some health- or social-policy studies may have been missed. The studies included were of a multidisciplinary nature and used varying methods and standards. Recognizing this limitation, and to ensure that our results were comparable among the included studies, we leveraged a detailed quality assessment tool and carefully evaluated the quality of studies. Although no formal language restrictions were applied, search terms were developed in English, which may introduce Anglophone bias.

sec.title: Results
sec.p:
The results of our search are presented in Fig. 1. Initial database searches identified 1165 references, from which 301 duplicates were removed. The remaining 864 references were reviewed for inclusion based on titles and abstracts. During this round of review, 820 studies were found to be irrelevant; 44 studies were included in the full-text screening.Fig. 1
PRISMA diagram of screening results.
Note: PRISMA = Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses.Fig. 1

During the full-text review, 16 studies were identified as gray literature or not peer-reviewed, 9 did not assess policy interventions, 6 did not report outcomes of interest, and 3 used study designs excluded from our review. The full text of 3 studies were unavailable and the full text of 1 study was not published in English. After full-text screening, 6 studies were included in our final review. Across the six included studies, most satisfied the core MMAT screening criteria, particularly with respect to clearly defined research questions and the use of data appropriate to address them. We interpreted the MMAT results at the level of individual domains rather than relying on total scores. The main areas of concern related to model specification, measurement assumptions, and analytical transparency. After MMAT assessment, no additional studies were excluded due to high risk or low quality. Results of the risk of bias exercise can be found in Appendix Table 1.

sec.title: Characteristics of included studies
sec.p:
A summary of key data extracted from each study included in our review is provided in Table 2. Descriptive characteristics were extracted from the final six included studies (see Table 3 for the details of included studies). Notably, there was pronounced heterogeneity in study methodology among the included studies. Three studies assessed policies across a grouping of countries, including subsets of Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) countries and lower-income countries. The remaining three studies focused on the economy of individual countries: individual countries studied were France (Chojnicki & Rabesandratana, 2017), Czech Republic (Gawthorpe, 2022) and Tunisia (Abdessalem & Cherni, 2016). Two of the studies assessed interventions and economic impacts in developing countries (Abdessalem & Cherni, 2016; Lai & Yip, 2022).Table 3
Details from included studies.Table 3
REFERENCE INFORMATIONSETTINGS AND METHODSKEY FINDINGSSUM SCORE
TitleAuthor (year)Country origin of dataAim/purposeStudy designDetails of intervention or policyTimeline of analysisStudy populationOutcome measureResultsType of motivationQuality of study (MMAT score)
Macroeconomic effects of pension reforms in the context of aging populations: Overlapping generations model simulations for TunisiaAbdessalem (2016) (Abdessalem & Cherni, 2016)TunisiaEvaluate effects of the demographic transition in Tunisia & discuss impacts of pension reformsQuantitative, descriptive. Computable general equilibrium model with an overlapping generations structure (CGEM-OLG)Reforms for pay-as-you-go (PAYG) pension systems:(1) Parametric reforms: contribution rate increase, pension level reduction, & increasing retirement age (60 to 65 years)(2) Structural reform: creating a mixed system with the introduction of a second funded pension pillar beside the PAYG system2005–2060Tunisian household generations for which each generation lives 74 years & age of retirement is 60 yearsGross domestic product (GDP)Decreased replacement rate lessens the gap between benefits and contributions reaching a maximum of 32% and a long-term value around 25% (PAYG system would alleviate economic repercussions); increased retirement age results in a decline compared to the baseline: the maximum value is 27.7% and binds to a long-term value close to 21.7%. An increase in the retirement age seems to be most effective in reducing the need for higher future contribution rates.Extrinsic6
Aging, human capital, and productivity in France: A generational accounting perspectiveChojnicki (2018) (Chojnicki & Rabesandratana, 2017)FranceAssess potential productivity gains resulting from improvements in educational attainment and health status of the working-age populationQuantitative, descriptive. Generational accounting modelInvestments in human capital including increasing educational attainment and improving health of adults2007–2060French population for which the life expectancy increases 77.2 to 86.0 for men and 84.2 to 91.1 for women; fertility rate is 1.98 children per woman between 2007 and 2015, then decreases to 1.95 children after; and 100,000/year immigrate to FranceGDPImproving educational attainment is not enough if fiscal policies are maintained but balance could be restored via tax adjustment; improved health status alone is also insufficient and would result in a long-run budgetary deficit.Intrinsic7
Population ageing and economic growth in seven OECD countriesFougere (1999) (Fougère & Mérette, 1999)Canada, France, Italy, Japan, Sweden, the United Kingdom, & the United StatesInvestigate the effects of ageing on economic growth and outputQuantitative, descriptive — overlapping-generations modelsInvestment in human capital1954–2082Populations aged 16–75 years old, assumed to retire at 63GDPWith human capital investment, there is an increase in labor supply and in average annual rates of economic growth ranges from 0.1% points for the United Kingdom, to 0.5 and 0.6% points for Japan and Italy (theoretically driven by reallocated portfolios and changes in investment portfolios across generations).Intrinsic7
Population ageing and sustainable fiscal policy in CzechiaGawthorpe (2022) (Gawthorpe, 2022)Czech RepublicAnalyze fiscal measures (postponing retirement, increasing pensions, and reducing social-security payments) to stabilize ageing impact on income and well-being and motivate decision-makers to use an extended fiscal modelQuantitative, descriptive—Dynamic Stochastic General Equilibrium (DSGE) modelPostponing retirement, increasing pensions, and reducing social-security payments2019–2037Czech populationLabour incomePostponing retirement and increasing pensions results suggested this would be effective only for well-being or income, not both; postponing retirement demonstrated lower productivity among older adults creating a labour supply shift to maintain well-being; increasing pensions would be most cost-effective if targeting well-being vs. income; reduced social security showed a negative shock for the social-security payments conditional on income stability.Extrinsic4
The role of older workers in population aging–economic growth nexus: Evidence from developing countriesLai (2022) (Lai & Yip, 2022)74 developing countriesInvestigate the role of labor force participation of older people in mitigating the negative impact of population aging on economic growthQuantitative, descriptive — static panel estimator (pooled ordinary least square (POLS), fixed effect model (FEM), and random effect model (REM))Increasing retirement age1990–2019Country populations in which "older persons" entails 65 and aboveGDPNegative impact of population aging on economic growth switches to positive w/rising labor force participation of older persons; proportion of the population 65+, old-age dependency ratio, & aging index were highly correlated.Extrinsic7
The greying Population: A wasted human capital or just a social liability?Spiezia (2002) (Spiezia, 2008)France, Germany, Italy, Japan, United Kingdom, and United StatesAnalyze the labour market effects of ageing & discuss the main policy measures proposed to copeQuantitative, descriptive—simulation modelRetirement-system reforms, pension reforms, educational attainment2000–2050Country populations for which “older persons” means older than 64 yearsTFPProposed reforms of pension schemes are likely to increase old-age labour force participation rates, but they will not automatically translate into higher employment without profound changes in the organization of work within firms. To increase job opportunities for elderly individuals, their careers need to be managed over their entire working lives so as to move older workers progressively from age-impaired work to age-neutral or age-enhanced work. Training would clearly have a central role to play in ensuring the continuous integration of the know-how of individuals into the organizational knowledge of firms.Extrinsic and intrinsic6
Note: GDP = gross domestic product; PAYG = pay as you go; TFP = total factor productivity.

Studies were required to use a quantitative study design to meet our inclusion criteria. A range of models was used to understand the effect of policies related to population aging on economic outcomes. These models include an overlapping generations model (two articles, 33%) (Abdessalem & Cherni, 2016; Fougère & Mérette, 1999), and one article each for a generational accounting model (Chojnicki & Rabesandratana, 2017), a dynamic stochastic general equilibrium model (Gawthorpe, 2022), a pooled ordinary least squares fixed-effect model/random effect model (Lai & Yip, 2022), and a simulation. The timeline for analysis ranged from 18 to 128 years, although a majority of the studies assessed changes over 50 to 70 years (four studies, 66%) (Abdessalem & Cherni, 2016; Chojnicki & Rabesandratana, 2017; Milanez, 2020; Spiezia, 2008). The most commonly reported outcome measure was GDP (four studies; 66%) (Abdessalem & Cherni, 2016; Chojnicki & Rabesandratana, 2017; Fougère & Mérette, 1999; Lai & Yip, 2022). Total factor productivity (TFP) (Spiezia, 2008) and income were each reported as outcome measures in one study (Gawthorpe, 2022).

The policies evaluated in the studies included in our review all focused on increasing labor force participation of older adults. Our review identified and categorized two primary mechanisms for how policy interventions may work with the aging population to address growing economic challenges: policies were categorized as either extrinsic or intrinsic motivators (see Table 4). Policies were classified as primarily intrinsic or extrinsic based on the dominant mechanism through which they were theorized to influence labor-market behavior, whether by altering internal drivers such as autonomy, competence, and perceived meaningfulness of work, or by modifying external incentives such as financial benefits, eligibility rules, or regulatory constraints. Although several interventions contain elements of both, policies were classified according to the primary mechanism, with the classification intended as an analytic heuristic rather than a rigid dichotomy (see Table 5).Table 4
Interventions identified in the review, based on motivation factor.Table 4
Intrinsic motivatorsExtrinsic motivators
Improved educational attainmentPostponement of retirement
Improved health attainmentReduction of social security
Relevant training and upskillingIncreasing pension over timeTable 5
Decision-oriented matrix.Table 5
StudyInterventionDirection of EffectGrowth or Productivity EndpointTime HorizonFeasibilityLikely tradeoffs (Participation)Likely tradeoffs (Productivity)
Abdessalem (2016) (Abdessalem & Cherni, 2016)Reforms for pay-as-you-go (PAYG) pension systems increase contribution rate; decrease pension level; increase retirement age; complementary funded pensionMixed effects: retirement-age increase least distortionary; funded pillar increases capital growthGDP growth; saving rate; capitalProjections to 2060Raising retirement age most feasible; funded pensions require strong fiscal environmentLonger working lives increases participation; lower pensions reduce retiree welfareFunded pillar improves capital leads to higher productivity
Chojnicki (2018) (Chojnicki & Rabesandratana, 2017)Investments in human capital including increasing educational attainment and improving health of adultsHuman-capital rise leads to large productivity increases.Large long-term productivity gains; potential 79% reduction in long-run fiscal burdenLabor productivity via skill composition and adult survivalProjections to 2060Education policy expansion is feasible but costly; health improvements variable, long implementation timelinesEducation may delay entry into workforceHigher skills and health raise productivity
Fougere (1999) (Fougère & Mérette, 1999)Tax-financed PAYG adjustment to maintain pension solvencyAging reduces savings and GDP; solvency requires higher contributionsGDP growth; capital accumulation; savings rates1954- 1999PAYG adjustment feasible but politically difficult (tax increases, lower disposable income)Higher contributions reduce disposable income and labor incentivesLower savings result in reduced capital accumulation and declined productivity
Gawthorpe (2022) (Gawthorpe, 2022)Postponing retirement, increasing pensions, and reducing social-security paymentsRetirement-age increase stabilizes income; pension increase stabilizes well-being; social-security reduction is most fiscally sustainableAggregate labor income & total well-beingForward projectionsPension reform politically difficult; social-security cuts more feasibleLower benefits may reduce incentives; later retirement increases supplyLower benefits stimulate earlier/larger labor supply response
Lai (2022) (Lai & Yip, 2022)Increasing retirement ageHigher participation offsets aging's negative effect on GDP per capitaGDP per capita growth rate1990-2019Feasible where age discrimination is addressed; requires institutional changesMay require incentives and institutional reforms; risk of unequal inclusionIncreased working population directly enhances output
Spiezia (2002) (Spiezia, 2008)Workplace redesign; health & ergonomic improvements; organization changes to maintain older-worker productivityProductivity of older workers can match younger workers under good conditionsWorker productivity levels2000-2050High feasibility; depends on employer adoption of ergonomic and organizational changeImproved work design supports retentionProductivity maintained via job redesign

Policies that serve as extrinsic motivators are defined as policies that aim to augment the participation of individuals in the workforce using external rewards or consequences, such as financial incentives or penalties. These extrinsic motivation-focused policies can directly increase labor force participation by leveraging official rules and regulations to legally mandate a later retirement age. Policies can also indirectly motivate individuals to remain in the workforce for a longer duration by reducing social security or increasing the amount of pension over time.

Policies that serve as intrinsic motivators are those that focus on interacting with the aging population to cultivate behavior which aligns an individual's internal values and satisfaction with the willingness to remain in the workforce. Individuals are thus driven by personal purpose and fulfillment rather than external rewards or punishments. These policies include improving educational attainment and relevant training as well as upskilling, increasing labor capacity and productivity, and boosting personal gratification from working. One such policy also includes ameliorating health attainment, making it more likely for individuals to find labor force participation comfortable and enjoyable.

sec.title: Extrinsic motivators
sec.p: Policies utilizing extrinsic motivators were described and evaluated in four studies. The most common intervention was postponing retirement age to induce older individuals to work longer and remain active in the labor market (Abdessalem & Cherni, 2016; Gawthorpe, 2022; Lai & Yip, 2022; Spiezia, 2008). Other approaches identified in our review included increasing pensions and reducing social security payments, both of which may modify the incentive to retire early that is associated with younger eligibility ages and flat accrual rates.

Results showed that the negative association between population aging and economic growth appeared to be mitigated by the labor force participation of older individuals (Lai & Yip, 2022). However, one study found that postponing retirement resulted in lower productivity among older adults (Gawthorpe, 2022). In this case, increasing pensions was reported to be superior at targeting well-being, although reducing the amount of social security payments was the most fiscally sustainable tool to achieve both income and well-being goals.

sec.title: Intrinsic motivators
sec.p: Our review identified three studies that described intrinsic motivators used to encourage populations to remain in the workplace and increase productivity gains. All the policies included a focus on increasing human capital using interventions that ranged from equipping the aging population with better resources by improving their health or equipping them with relevant skills to improved educational attainment—both of which, in turn, would improve labor supply. By improving personal attributes that are useful for production processes and more appropriate for future changes in population skill structure, workers may find it more enjoyable and gratifying to remain in the workforce and, consequently, may be less likely to be discouraged and exit the labor market.

Incentivizing human capital accumulation and reallocating investments from physical capital to human capital can have generational effects and lead to higher per capita output of later generations, where investments into additional educational attainment have been made (Lai & Yip, 2022). One study found that annual rates of economic growth can be improved with human capital investment, driven by cross-generational investment portfolio changes (Fougère & Mérette, 1999). Improving health attainment was another way to drive economic sustainability. Augmenting health attainment was found to positively impact worker productivity (Chojnicki & Rabesandratana, 2017).

There are, nevertheless limitations for policies that aim to increase intrinsic motivation to incentivize individuals to remain in the workforce. One study found that intrinsic motivators alone were not enough to prevent long-term budgetary deficits in countries faced with aging populations (Chojnicki & Rabesandratana, 2017). However, that same study indicated the extent to which the long-term effects of aging on fiscal policy are sensitive to the assumptions made regarding the accumulation of human capital—a factor that requires further research (Chojnicki & Rabesandratana, 2017).

Table 4 presents a decision-oriented synthesis of the included studies, outlining the policy interventions examined, their effects on economic growth or productivity, and the key considerations for feasibility and tradeoffs.

Several types of policy interventions were reviewed to mitigate the economic consequences of population aging, including (1) increasing labor force participation among older adults—whether through formal retirement-age extensions, reduced social-security replacement rates, or improved workplace design, (2) human-capital enhancing policies and (3) pension and tax-based reforms.

sec.title: Discussion and Conclusion
sec.p: Countries around the world are facing aging populations and higher dependency ratios. This population age structure is likely to impact labor market supply and demand as well as economic performance. Importantly, with an aging population, governments will have to cope with the challenge of a potential reduction in revenue and the simultaneous need to expand health care, pensions, and social protection.

This review examined the evidence on policy interventions designed to mitigate the economic effects of population aging. We leveraged the self-determination theory framework to define intrinsic and extrinsic sources of motivation and to describe and summarize the published evidence of policy interventions to ameliorate the effects of aging populations on economic productivity. Although many governments rely heavily on raising retirement ages and adjusting pension parameters, the available modeling evidence suggests that such extrinsic approaches may increase labor force participation without necessarily improving productivity.

However, the evidence base remains limited. Only six modeling studies met the inclusion criteria, underscoring the nascency of this research area and substantial heterogeneity in measurement approaches. Results from our study should therefore be interpreted with caution due to the limited evidence base. Nonetheless, a systematic review is crucial to map the existing evidence, reveal methodological patterns, and guide the development of a more robust empirical literature.

Despite the limited evidence base, several consistent patterns emerge. Taken together, the current evidence offers three policy-relevant insights that may inform governments navigating demographic aging. First, for countries relying primarily on raising retirement age or adjusting pension parameters, the available evidence suggests that these strategies may stabilize labor supply but are less likely to sustain productivity on their own. Policies that extend working lives through extrinsic incentives—such as postponing retirement age, reducing replacement rates, or modifying contribution structures—appear capable of increasing participation among older workers. However, several studies indicate that gains in participation do not automatically translate into higher productivity, and in some cases may be associated with declining output per worker. These findings suggest that parametric pension reforms may provide short-term fiscal relief but should not be implemented as stand-alone solutions for maintaining long-run economic performance in aging societies.

Second, policies that enhance human capital across the life course are likely to yield long-run returns. Interventions that improve health status, educational attainment, and opportunities for reskilling or workplace adaptation were consistently associated with positive productivity trajectories. From a self-determination theory perspective, such policies may operate by strengthening intrinsic motivation—enabling older adults to remain engaged in work because it remains feasible, meaningful, and aligned with evolving capabilities. Although the empirical base remains limited, the reviewed studies collectively suggest that investments made earlier in the life course may shape labor-market adaptability decades later.

Third, substantial evidence gaps remain, particularly for sector-specific productivity outcomes. Most included studies relied on macroeconomic modeling frameworks and focused on aggregate GDP or labor-supply indicators, leaving important questions unanswered about how aging interacts with productivity across different sectors, occupational structures, and health-system contexts. Evidence from low- and middle-income countries remains sparse, despite the rapid demographic transition in many of these settings. Future research would benefit from integrating sector-level productivity measures, workforce organization dynamics, and demand-side factors to better inform policy design in diverse economic environments.

Overall, the current body of evidence suggests that policies focused exclusively on tightening pension systems or extending retirement ages may offer fiscal stabilization but are less likely to generate sustained productivity gains without complementary investments in human capital and workplace adaptation. Globally, as population aging accelerates, policymakers may need to shift from a narrow focus on retirement parameters toward broader strategies that support lifelong capability development, age-inclusive work design, and context-specific policy experimentation.

Consistent with broader literature on incentives, policies that rely primarily on externally imposed labor-supply extensions may carry unintended productivity trade-offs, whereas approaches that combine financial incentives with supportive work environments appear more likely to balance participation and productivity goals. Across the reviewed studies, extrinsic strategies that emphasized positive reinforcement—such as pension rewards for delayed retirement — tended to align more closely with well-being or productivity outcomes than restrictive measures that shortened retirement duration or reduced benefits (Lin et al., 2022).

Policies aimed at strengthening intrinsic motivation—primarily through investments in health, education, skills, and workplace design—appear more promising for long-run productivity by supporting sustained engagement rather than purely extending tenure. This finding aligns with the literature suggesting that contexts that support human autonomy and competence are conducive to meeting workers’ need for increased internalization and integration (Ryan & Deci, 2000). It is further congruent with meta-analysis results demonstrating a positive relationship between aging and intrinsic motivation (Kooij et al., 2011). Benefits of intrinsic motivation may extend beyond retention alone. Specifically, these results support existing evidence that shows that intrinsic motivation more successfully enhances the performance of healthcare workers (Lin et al., 2022).

Policies that reduce structural or workplace barriers to extended working lives offer immediate, politically feasible benefits, while human-capital policies provide the largest long-run productivity gains. By contrast, tax- and contribution-based adjustments alone are unlikely to sustain growth and may introduce counterproductive distortions.

Our review only identified policies that worked on a single supply-side lever of the issue of population aging: workforce participation. We did not evaluate evidence regarding policy interventions in caretaking or social services that may address the growing demand side issues of providing services for aging populations. Effectively addressing the challenge of aging populations will likely require a multi-faceted approach that tackles both demand and supply challenges. Delivering care to a growing number of older individuals will require a robust care workforce and care system that extends beyond the current capacities of governments worldwide. Thus, further investigations should be conducted to assess the capacity of policy interventions to address aging issues with alternative levers that could reinforce public systems for health care, pensions, and social protection.

One potential policy that may address the negative impact of an aging population on the economy, but has not yet been broadly considered, implemented, and studied to allow causal inference, is reinforcing an older adult caretaking workforce. One study conducted in Taiwan provided preliminary evidence to suggest that expanding the older adult caretaking workforce can create more job opportunities, stimulate the economy, and dampen the negative effects of an aging population on the economy (Huang et al., 2019). However, the analysis conducted does not allow for the evaluation of causal inference, so this study was not included in our review. Future research should aim to identify factors with evidence to suggest the beneficial effect of older adult caretaking workforce on the economy amid an aging population.

Evidence from a small number of modeling studies suggests that policies aimed at mitigating the negative effects of population aging on economic growth and productivity, particularly those that cultivate intrinsic motivation among older adults to remain in the workforce and reduce structural or workplace barriers, may serve as important policy tools. This assertion aligns with recent literature suggesting that age-supportive employment policies bolster work engagement and sustainable employment when older workers consider their job to have intrinsic value (Kooij et al., 2011; Truxillo et al., 2015; van Dam & Kemps, 2017; Yaldiz et al., 2017). However, intrinsic motivations may be very context specific. For this reason, evidence illustrating the effectiveness of specific policies to promote intrinsic motivation should be interpreted with caution and efforts should be made to cultivate local evidence when possible. Identified policies with evidence to support their effectiveness should not be adopted as one-size-fits-all solutions. Instead, these policies should be piloted thoroughly, taking specific population and worker preference and variation into consideration within a local context.

sec.title: CRediT authorship contribution statement
sec.p: Tracy Kuo Lin: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Resources, Investigation, Conceptualization. Kalin Werner: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Ashley Mitchell: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Formal analysis, Data curation. Mariam M. Hamza: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Conceptualization. Gustavo De Marco: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Christopher H. Herbst: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

sec.title: Ethical statement
sec.p: This study used publicly available, de-identified secondary data and did not require ethical approval. The authors affirm that this manuscript complies with all ethical standards required by SSM - Population Health.

sec.title: Funding
sec.p: The project is funded by World Bank Human Development in the GCC region. The findings, interpretations, and conclusions expressed in this work are those of the authors, and do not necessarily reflect the views of The World Bank, their Boards of Directors, or the governments they represent.

sec.title: Declaration of competing interest
sec.p: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sec.title: Data availability
sec.p: Data will be made available on request.

ack.title: Acknowledgement
ack.p: The team is grateful for the overall support provided by Fadia M. Saadah, World Bank Director for Human Development; Michele Gragnolati, World Bank Practice Manager of HNP in MENA and Safaa El-Kogali, World Bank Country Director for the GCC countries.
